findings.title: Duplicate trigger phrase across multiple agents
findings.snippet: user: "Analyze this codebase" (code-analyzer, report-reconciler, report-critic, plan-critic)
findings.description: Four agents (code-analyzer, report-reconciler, report-critic, plan-critic) use the exact phrase 'Analyze this codebase' as the user turn in at least one example block. This creates ambiguity in agent triggering since Claude cannot distinguish which agent to dispatch based on identical user input. Each agent serves a distinct role in the pipeline but the examples do not differentiate the triggering conditions.
findings.recommendation: Differentiate the user turns in example blocks to reflect each agent's actual trigger point. For example: code-analyzer could use 'Scan this project for security issues', report-reconciler could use 'Merge the scan findings into a report', report-critic could use 'Validate the analysis report quality', and plan-critic could use 'Check the refactoring plan for feasibility'.

findings.title: code-analyzer body delegates entirely to sub-skills without inline guardrails
findings.snippet:
You are a Code Analyzer. You run ONE analysis dimension against a codebase and return structured findings.

## Input

You will receive:
- A target path to analyze
...
findings.description: The code-analyzer system prompt body describes a dispatch process (detect stack, load sub-skill, execute scan, return findings) but does not include inline quality standards or error handling guardrails. It relies entirely on the loaded sub-skill for behavioral boundaries. The new Bash Usage Constraints section (lines 109-116) is a positive addition, but the agent still lacks a Quality Standards section and an Error Handling section. The agent conventions profile recommends that system prompts include explicit quality standards and output format sections.
findings.recommendation: Add a minimal 'Quality Standards' section (e.g., 'Every finding MUST include all required schema fields', 'Return empty findings array rather than failing silently') and an 'Error Handling' section (e.g., 'If sub-skill cannot be loaded, return a single critical finding explaining the failure') directly in the system prompt body.

findings.title: Duplicate cyan color across code-analyzer and report-critic
findings.description: After the report-critic color change from yellow to cyan, two agents now share the same color (cyan). The agent conventions profile states that agents within the same plugin should use distinct colors to aid visual identification. Having both code-analyzer and report-critic as cyan reduces the visual differentiation benefit of color assignments.
findings.recommendation: Restore report-critic to yellow (semantically appropriate for validation/quality-check agents) or assign a different unused color. Alternatively, change code-analyzer to a different color if cyan is preferred for the critic.

findings.title: report-critic color cyan misaligns with validation role
findings.description: The report-critic agent performs validation, quality checking, and pass/fail verdicts — tasks that semantically align with yellow (validation, caution, quality checks) per the color conventions. The current cyan color semantically maps to 'data processing, secondary analysis, transformation', which does not match the critic's primary role. The prior assignment of yellow was a better semantic fit.
findings.recommendation: Change the color back to yellow, which aligns with the agent's validation and quality-checking role per the semantic color table in the agent conventions profile.

findings.title: code-analyzer examples cover limited scenario range
findings.snippet:
<example>
Context: Orchestrator dispatches parallel dimension scans
user: "Analyze this codebase"
...
<example>
Context: User wants a single dimension scan
user: "Just scan the security of this project"
findings.description: Both examples show the agent being triggered by direct analysis requests. Neither demonstrates edge cases such as plugin-mode dispatch, error recovery, or partial scan completion. The examples share similar phrasing patterns and both describe orchestrator-driven usage.
findings.recommendation: Add a third example showing plugin-mode dispatch or a scenario where stack auto-detection is needed, to demonstrate the agent's full behavioral range.

findings.title: report-critic examples cover limited scenario range
findings.snippet:
<example>
Context: Orchestrator has a reconciled report ready for validation
user: "Analyze this codebase"
...
<example>
Context: Report has a scoring inconsistency
user: "Check this analysis report for scoring errors"
findings.description: Both examples focus on initial validation. Neither shows the iteration > 1 case where prior feedback is provided and the critic must check whether previously-blocking issues were resolved. This is a core workflow documented in the system prompt body.
findings.recommendation: Add a third example demonstrating the iteration > 1 feedback loop, where the critic receives prior feedback and evaluates whether revisions addressed blocking issues.

findings.title: refactoring-planner examples cover limited scenario range
findings.snippet:
<example>
Context: User has prior analysis results and wants fresh plans
user: "Generate refactoring plans from the latest analysis"
...
<example>
Context: User wants to regenerate plans with different priorities
user: "Regenerate the plans but prioritize security over architecture"
findings.description: Both examples show user-initiated plan generation. Neither demonstrates the orchestrator dispatch pattern or the critic-feedback revision workflow (Step 4.5), which are documented capabilities in the system prompt body.
findings.recommendation: Add a third example showing the orchestrator dispatching the planner with cross-analysis results and/or critic feedback for revision.

findings.title: report-reconciler missing edge-case guidance
findings.description: The report-reconciler system prompt body describes two modes and a revision handling section but lacks an explicit edge-case or error-handling section. It does not address scenarios like: empty findings from all scanners, malformed finding JSON, or conflicting dedup decisions.
findings.recommendation: Add an 'Edge Cases' section covering: all scanners return empty findings, malformed JSON in dimension findings, dedup conflicts where findings overlap but have different severities.

findings.title: refactoring-planner missing quality standards section
findings.description: The refactoring-planner system prompt body has no 'Quality Standards' or 'Quality Criteria' section. While it describes a process and now includes an Output Format section, it does not specify what constitutes a high-quality plan output.
findings.recommendation: Add a brief 'Quality Standards' section defining criteria such as: every critical/high finding must map to a plan step, effort estimates must be provided, phases must not exceed a maximum file-touch count.

findings.title: refactoring-planner missing edge-case guidance
findings.description: The refactoring-planner system prompt body contains no explicit edge-case or error-handling section. The Notes section mentions 'no prior analysis exists' but does not cover scenarios like empty findings, conflicting priority overrides, or malformed scan report JSON.
findings.recommendation: Add an 'Edge Cases' section similar to report-critic and plan-critic agents, covering: empty scan reports, malformed JSON input, conflicting dimension priorities, and missing cross-analysis data.

findings.title: No reactive triggering patterns in any agent
findings.description: All five agents' example blocks show only reactive patterns (user asks, agent responds). None demonstrate proactive triggering where the agent is invoked automatically by context (e.g., after code is written, after a file is saved). The agent conventions profile recommends showing both proactive and reactive triggering patterns when applicable.
findings.recommendation: For agents that can be proactively triggered (e.g., code-analyzer after code generation, report-critic after report reconciliation), add example blocks demonstrating proactive dispatch by the orchestrator.

findings.title: All agents use model inherit without differentiation
findings.description: All five agents declare model: inherit. While this is a valid and recommended default, the agent conventions profile suggests that simple validation agents (report-critic, plan-critic) could benefit from model: haiku for cost efficiency, while the code-analyzer (which performs complex reasoning) might warrant model: sonnet or opus. This is informational only; inherit is not wrong but may indicate unconsidered model selection.
findings.recommendation: Consider whether report-critic and plan-critic could use model: haiku (their tasks are structured validation) and whether code-analyzer benefits from a specific model for complex analysis reasoning. Document the rationale for inherit in each agent if it is intentional.

findings.title: Color diversity reduced by duplicate cyan assignment
findings.snippet: code-analyzer: cyan, report-reconciler: blue, report-critic: cyan, refactoring-planner: magenta, plan-critic: yellow
findings.description: The plugin now uses 4 distinct colors across 5 agents but with a duplicate: code-analyzer and report-critic both use cyan. Previously, 4 distinct colors were used with better semantic alignment (report-critic was yellow). The two critic agents no longer share yellow; plan-critic retains yellow while report-critic moved to cyan, reducing semantic consistency.
findings.recommendation: Restore report-critic to yellow to re-establish the prior color diversity and semantic alignment where both critics shared the validation color.

findings.title: All agents use directory-style agent format consistently
findings.description: All five agents consistently use the directory-style format (agents/{name}/AGENT.md) rather than mixing flat files and directories. This is a positive structural consistency pattern.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. Consistent directory-style format aids discoverability and supports future addition of agent-local supporting files.

findings.title: Example counts at minimum threshold across all agents
findings.description: All five agents declare exactly 2 example blocks each, which is at the minimum threshold per the agent conventions profile (recommended: 2-4). Official plugin best practices recommend 2-4 examples covering diverse scenarios. No agent has added a third example to demonstrate complex workflows such as plugin-mode dispatch, critic feedback loops, or error recovery.
findings.recommendation: Add a third example to agents with complex multi-path workflows (code-analyzer, report-critic, refactoring-planner) to improve trigger reliability and demonstrate edge-case behavior.

official_comparison.note: Official plugins index shows plugin-dev has 3 agents and pr-review-toolkit has 6 agents. This plugin's 5 agents with 2 examples each matches the typical pattern. All official plugins with agents use 0-6 agents. Example count of 2 per agent is at the minimum threshold; official best practices recommend 2-4.
official_comparison.example_count_assessment: At minimum threshold (2 per agent). Consider adding a third example to agents with complex workflows.
official_comparison.model_assessment: All agents use inherit, consistent with official plugin defaults. No unusual selections detected.
